title: AJs BTN — Squeeze vs UTG Open + CO Flat
prompt: $2/$5 live. UTG opens $20 (4x), CO flats. You're BTN with A♥J♥ (100bb). Squeeze, call, or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call (flat)
Squeeze to ~$75

answer.explanation: AJs is a premium squeeze candidate BTN vs opener + flat. CO is trapped; size to 15-18x to deny both players good odds and play HU IP.

A.explanation: Pool A may 4-bet; AJs has equity to call off a 4-bet IP. Squeezing is still primary.

B.explanation: Pool B passives rarely 4-bet. Size up to 18x to isolate UTG; CO almost always folds.

C.explanation: Pool C nits fold both seats most of the time — high fold equity makes this a slam-dunk squeeze.

common_mistakes: Cold-calling AJs and playing multiway with no initiative
Under-sizing the squeeze and getting called by CO with good odds
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Live Pool B flatters rarely continue vs squeezes. Squeeze big to isolate UTG and play HU in position.
coaching_context.key_concept: AJs is a premium squeeze candidate BTN vs opener + flat.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: AJs BTN — Squeeze vs UTG Open + CO Flat is difficult because many players default to cold-calling AJs and playing multiway with no initiative, even though AJs changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Squeeze to ~$75 works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What makes squeeze to ~$75 the highest-EV line here instead of taking the passive option?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The key is the preflop range and position dynamic: this spot gains too much from initiative, fold equity, isolation, or value extraction to passively continue.

options.label: Flatting or checking keeps weaker hands in, so aggression mostly burns value or fold equity.
The spot is driven by the preflop range and position dynamic, so building the pot or isolating now captures more value than a passive line.

title: 77 SB — Don't Squeeze Small Pair OOP
prompt: $2/$5 live. CO opens $15 (3x), BTN flats. You're SB with 7♥7♦ (100bb). Squeeze, call, or fold?

options.label: Fold
Call (complete)
Squeeze to ~$55

answer.explanation: 77 OOP vs opener + caller is a call or fold — not a squeeze. Small pairs are bad squeeze hands: poor fold equity, vulnerable to 4-bets, and need to set-mine when called.

A.explanation: Pool A 3-bets wide and 4-bets vs squeezes. 77 OOP vs a Pool A BTN flat is a fold — too much pressure post-flop.

B.explanation: Pool B passives rarely raise back. Call to set-mine multiway — you're getting implied odds against two calling stations.

C.explanation: Pool C nit flatters usually have strong hands. Set-mining odds are marginal OOP vs two tight players.

common_mistakes: Squeezing small pairs OOP thinking they're 'strong enough'
Not accounting for the 4-bet risk that 77 cannot profitably call off
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Small pairs OOP want to see cheap flops multiway, not build big pots vs two players out of position.
coaching_context.key_concept: 77 OOP vs opener + caller is a call or fold — not a squeeze. A shifts to fold; and C shifts to fold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: 77 SB — Don't Squeeze Small Pair OOP is difficult because many players default to squeezing small pairs OOP thinking they're 'strong enough', even though 77 changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Call (complete) works because 77 makes its money by realizing implied odds and getting paid when it connects cleanly, not by forcing a bigger pot now.

diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: This combo sits in the continue region because the preflop range and position dynamic keeps enough worse hands, bluffs, or realizable equity in play without needing to escalate the pot.

title: AQo BTN — 4-Bet or Fold vs UTG Open + CO 3-Bet
prompt: $2/$5 live. UTG opens $15, CO 3-bets to $50. You're BTN with A♠Q♣ (100bb). What's your action?

options.label: Fold
Cold-Call (flat)
4-Bet to ~$130

answer.explanation: Cold-calling a 3-bet squeeze (UTG + CO in pot) with AQo is the leak. 4-bet or fold — cold-calling puts you in a brutal spot multiway with no initiative.

A.explanation: Pool A CO 3-bets can include QQ+/AK and sometimes bluffs. 4-bet/fold with AQo — cold-calling is never correct here.

B.explanation: Pool B CO 3-bet range is very tight (QQ+/AK). AQo is dominated often enough that folding is fine. 4-betting is a reasonable bluff but marginal.

C.explanation: Pool C nit CO 3-bet = QQ+/AK almost always. Fold AQo — you're crushed by their range.

common_mistakes: Cold-calling a 3-bet IP with AQo 'just to see a flop'
Playing AQo passively between an opener and 3-bettor
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Cold-calling a 3-bet in a 3-way pot with AQo makes you the weakest player in the pot. 4-bet or fold.
coaching_context.key_concept: Cold-calling a 3-bet squeeze (UTG + CO in pot) with AQo is the leak. B shifts to fold; and C shifts to fold.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: AQo BTN — 4-Bet or Fold vs UTG Open + CO 3-Bet is difficult because many players default to cold-calling a 3-bet IP with AQo 'just to see a flop', even though AQo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: AQo 4-bets because cold-calling a squeeze between UTG and CO puts hero in a multiway pot OOP with no initiative — 4-betting forces villain to define their range and keeps the decision binary.

title: BB KJo — Probe Turn After BTN Checks Dry Flop
prompt: $2/$5 live. BTN opens, you call BB with K♠J♦. Flop T♥7♦2♣ — you check, BTN checks back. Turn Q♠. Do you lead or check?

options.label: Donk overbet bluff (65%+ pot)
Check (give free card)
Lead / Probe (40-50% pot)

answer.explanation: BTN checked back T72r — their range is capped. Q turn gives hero KJ an OESD (A or 9) plus two overcards. Probe to charge draws, deny free cards, and take down pots BTN checked back with air.

A.explanation: Pool A BTN may have slowplayed a set; check-calling is acceptable but leading with equity is preferred.

B.explanation: Pool B BTN checked back = weak. Probe confidently; they will fold air and call dominated hands.

C.explanation: Pool C nits check back weak top pairs and second pairs on dry flops. Probe to charge them or take it down.

common_mistakes: Checking and giving BTN a free card when their range is demonstrably capped
Only probing with made hands — KJo with OESD equity is a prime semi-bluff probe
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Live players check back flops with medium-strength hands and weak draws. BTN's check caps their range — probe and collect.
coaching_context.key_concept: BTN checked back T72r — their range is capped.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: BB KJo — Probe Turn After BTN Checks Dry Flop is difficult because many players default to checking and giving BTN a free card when their range is demonstrably capped, even though KJo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Lead / Probe (40-50% pot) works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

C.explanation: Pool C called twice = they have it (KJ+). Check back K9 — you're behind their range when they call twice.

common_mistakes: Checking back second pair vs a station who calls too wide
Betting too large and pricing out the calling range that makes the thin value profitable
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Thin value betting vs passive stations is a core live-cash skill. Size small, target their wide calling range, and collect extra chips.
coaching_context.key_concept: K9 on K-high with second pair is thin value vs a passive station. A shifts to check-back (pot control); and C shifts to check-back (pot control).
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Passive Station — Thin River Value with Second Pair is difficult because many players default to checking back second pair vs a station who calls too wide, even though K9o changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: K9 second pair bets small (30-40%) against a passive station who calls with Jx, 5x, and weak pairs — larger sizing loses value because stations fold their weakest holdings to pressure they perceive as strong.

title: Passive Station — Never Bluff, Check River with Air
prompt: $2/$5 live. You have T♣8♦ (air, no pair, missed draw). Station villain called flop + turn on A♠J♥6♦/2♣. River 3♠. Bluff or check?

options.label: Give up / Check-back
Bluff small (30% pot)
Bluff large (75%+ pot)

answer.explanation: Never bluff passive stations. They call 3 streets with bottom pair and gut-shots — any river bluff has zero fold equity. Check back and accept the loss. Bluffing stations is the #1 live-cash leak.

A.explanation: Even Pool A players who called twice have enough of a range to make a river bluff unprofitable here.

B.explanation: Pool B station called twice = they have a hand. Zero fold equity on the river. Check back immediately.

C.explanation: Pool C nit called twice = they have it. No bluff is profitable here.

common_mistakes: Firing a third barrel bluff vs a passive station who 'has to be weak'
Confusing frequency with profitability — even if stations fold sometimes, they don't fold enough
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Bluffing passive stations is the most expensive mistake in live cash. When you have air vs a station, check. Save bluffs for players who can fold.
coaching_context.key_concept: Never bluff passive stations.
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Passive Station — Never Bluff, Check River with Air is difficult because many players default to firing a third barrel bluff vs a passive station who 'has to be weak', even though T8o changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Give up / Check-back works because it isolates the weaker part of villain's range or denies equity before that range gets to realize too cheaply.

title: Aggro Rec — Call Down 3 Barrels with Bluff Catcher
prompt: $2/$5 live. Aggro rec fired all 3 streets on Q♥J♦4♣/T♠/2♣. You have Q♠8♦ (top pair). Villain bets 75% pot river. Call or fold?

options.label: Fold (over-fold)
Call down
Check-raise (bluff)

answer.explanation: Aggro recs fire all 3 streets with a bluff frequency far above GTO. Q♠8♦ on Q-J-4/T/2 (no flush, no straight) is a clear bluff catcher. Call down — their bluffing range makes this profitable.

A.explanation: Pool A BTN has value here too (KK, KQ, AQ, KJ). Q8 is borderline — calling is fine but not as clear as vs aggro recs.

B.explanation: Pool B passive BTN 3-barrels = value. Fold Q8 vs a passive player who never bluffs this line.

C.explanation: Pool C nit 3-barrel = strong. Fold Q8 without hesitation.

common_mistakes: Folding top pair vs aggro recs who over-bluff because 'they could have it'
Not adjusting bluff-catching thresholds based on villain type
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Aggro recs over-bluff by 2-3x GTO frequency. Call wider than you think vs their 3-barrel lines. Top pair is a standard call.
coaching_context.key_concept: Aggro recs fire all 3 streets with a bluff frequency far above GTO. A shifts to fold (over-fold); B shifts to fold (over-fold); and C shifts to fold (over-fold).
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Aggro Rec — Call Down 3 Barrels with Bluff Catcher is difficult because many players default to folding top pair vs aggro recs who over-bluff because 'they could have it', even though Q8o changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Call down works because Q8o lands on the right side of the value-versus-bluff threshold for the exact line described in the drill.

title: Aggro Rec Checks River — Bet for Value After Trap
prompt: $2/$5 live. Aggro rec bet flop + turn on K♦T♣5♠/8♣, then checks river J♥. You have K♠Q♥. Bet or check?

options.label: Check-back (induce)
Check-back (pot control)
Bet for value (50-65%)

answer.explanation: Aggro recs who check rivers after barreling twice usually gave up with a bluff or have a medium-strength hand. KQo TPTK bets for value — they call with worse Kings, pairs, and busted draws.

A.explanation: Pool A checks back to control pot or trap. KQ value bet is solid but check-back is acceptable.

B.explanation: Pool B check = gave up or trap. KQ is thin value bet; checking is fine if villain may CR.

C.explanation: Pool C 2-barrel then check = trap with strong hand. Check-back KQ to avoid a raise.

common_mistakes: Checking back TPTK when aggro rec checks river — missing thin value from their busted draws
Fearing an aggro rec check-raise when they showed weakness by checking
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: When aggro recs check the river after 2 barrels, they often gave up. Bet for thin value with top pair — extract from their wide calling range.
coaching_context.key_concept: Aggro recs who check rivers after barreling twice usually gave up with a bluff or have a medium-strength hand. B shifts to check-back (pot control); and C shifts to check-back (pot control).
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Aggro Rec Checks River — Bet for Value After Trap is difficult because many players default to checking back TPTK when aggro rec checks river — missing thin value from their busted draws, even though KQo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Bet for value (50-65%) works because KQo lands on the right side of the value-versus-bluff threshold for the exact line described in the drill.

title: Aggro Rec Overbet River — Call Down with Bluff Catcher
prompt: $2/$5 live. Aggro rec overbets river (150% pot) on A♠K♦Q♣/7♠/5♦. You have A♥J♣ (TPTK). Fold or call?

options.label: Fold (give up)
Call (bluff catcher)
Raise (re-bluff)

answer.explanation: Aggro rec bet flop, checked turn, then overbets river — this is a classic aggro rec bluff pattern. AJo TPTK on AKQ75 is a strong bluff catcher. Call down and punish their over-bluffing river range.

A.explanation: Pool A BTN x-turn then overbet-river is polarized; AJ calls for bluff-catching value.

B.explanation: Pool B passive x-turn then overbet = JT (straight) or AA/KK. Fold AJ — this is a monster from a passive player.

C.explanation: Pool C nit x-turn then overbet = nuts. Fold AJ without hesitation.

common_mistakes: Folding top pair vs aggro recs who over-bluff river overbets
Not adjusting to player type — AJ is a fold vs passive player but a call vs aggro rec
coaching_context.live_pool_notes: Aggro recs love pot-sized river bluffs with missed draws. AJo on AKQ75 is a clear call vs an identified over-bluffer. Trap and let them bluff.
coaching_context.key_concept: Aggro rec bet flop, checked turn, then overbets river — this is a classic aggro rec bluff pattern. B shifts to fold (give up); and C shifts to fold (give up).
coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Aggro Rec Overbet River — Call Down with Bluff Catcher is difficult because many players default to folding top pair vs aggro recs who over-bluff river overbets, even though AJo changes value once you follow the actual line and pool assumptions in the drill.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Call (bluff catcher) works because AJo lands on the right side of the value-versus-bluff threshold for the exact line described in the drill.
